1: 'For behold, the day is coming, burning like an oven, when all the arrogant and all evildoers will be stubble. The day that is coming shall set them ablaze, says YHWH of hosts, so that it will leave them neither root nor branch.' The Day of the Lord as consuming fire — the oven (tanur) that burns the stubble (qash). The arrogant and evildoers of 3:15 become the dry stubble of judgment. Neither root nor branch: complete extermination of the line — the genealogical language of covenant curse (cutting off descendants). The image of fire consuming stubble is the inverse of the sun of righteousness rising with healing wings (v. 2).
2: 'But for you who fear my name, the sun of righteousness shall rise with healing in its wings. You shall go out leaping like calves from the stall.' The faithful remnant's experience of the Day: the sun of righteousness (shemesh tzedaqah) rising with healing in its wings (umarpeh bikhnafeiha). The winged sun was a widespread ancient Near Eastern royal symbol — on Egyptian and Mesopotamian monuments, the winged sun disk represented divine sovereign protection. Here YHWH appropriates the symbol: the rising of his righteousness is like a solar disk with protecting, healing wings. The calves leaping from the stall — the image of restored joy and freedom, the opposite of the mourning walk of 3:14.
3: 'And you shall tread down the wicked, for they will be ashes under the soles of your feet, on the day when I act, says YHWH of hosts.' The reversal of fortunes: the wicked who prospered while the righteous walked in mourning will be ashes under the feet of the righteous. The day of action (yom asher ani oseh) — the Day when YHWH acts decisively, not merely providentially. The ashes image connects to v. 1 (the burning oven that leaves nothing): the fire has done its work, leaving only ashes for the faithful to walk over.
4: 'Remember the law of my servant Moses, the statutes and rules that I commanded him at Horeb for all Israel.' The final call to Torah observance — all of Moses' law, given at Horeb (Sinai). This verse brackets the OT canon: the Torah (Moses, Horeb) is named explicitly as the foundation before the final prophetic word. The remembrance (zichru Torat Moshe avdi) bridges the entire Mosaic covenant tradition with the prophetic tradition that Malachi represents.
5: 'Behold, I will send you Elijah the prophet before the great and awesome day of YHWH comes.' The Elijah promise: YHWH will send Elijah (Eliyahu hanavi) before the Day of the Lord. This is the explicit basis for Jesus's identification of John the Baptist as the coming Elijah (Matt 11:14; 17:12-13; Mark 9:11-13; Luke 1:17). Elijah was the greatest of the pre-classical prophets — the one taken up to heaven in a whirlwind (2 Kgs 2:11) without death, the one expected to return. His return before the Day is the final sign that the eschatological hour has arrived.
6: 'And he will turn the hearts of fathers to their children and the hearts of children to their fathers, lest I come and strike the land with a decree of utter destruction.' The Elijah-messenger's mission: the reconciliation of generations (veheshiv lev avot al banim velev banim al avotam). The turning of fathers to children and children to fathers — the repair of the fractured covenant community across its generational lines. The intergenerational fracture (intermarriage with foreign wives, divorce of first wives, contempt for the tradition) is the disease; generational reconciliation is the cure. The final warning: if this reconciliation does not happen, YHWH will strike the land with cherem (utter destruction/ban) — the most severe covenant curse. The OT ends with a threat and a hope: the Elijah who is coming can prevent the final judgment if the people respond. The NT presents John the Baptist's ministry as the fulfillment of this hope — and Jesus's coming as the answer to the warning.
